coredata.dc:title: Repurposing the domestic organic waste into green emissive carbon dots and carbonized adsorbent: A sustainable zero waste process for metal sensing and dye sequestration
coredata.dc:creator: Das, Manita
Thakkar, Harshil
Patel, Divyesh
Thakore, Sonal
coredata.dc:description: This work reports a one pot facile route for complete valorization of domestic organic waste into biogenic carbon dots (BCDs) and biochar for application as nanosensor and adsorbent respectively. Among the various precursors used, dried rose petals derived (BCDRP) and ripe banana peel derived (BCDBP) carbon dots demonstrated quantum yields comparable to other previously reported biomass derived green fluorescing carbon dots. The BCDs exhibited sizes < 10 nm and excellent photo-stability upto 30 days with respect to different irradiation times as well as ionic strength. The BCDs were assessed for their optical performance and employed as fluorescent sensor for detection of heavy metal ions. The semi-quantitative and quantitative studies shows that BCDRP was selective towards Cr6+ whereas BCDBP detected Fe3+ in a mixture of metal ions. LOD values in pM range (81 pM for Cr6+ in presence of BCDRP and 121 pM for Fe3+ in presence of BCDBP) advocates their high sensitivity. The real sample analyzing potential of the BCDs with excellent recoveries and minimal RSD suggest their successful practical applicability. For circular economy, the biochar formed as a byproduct was reused as an adsorbent for removal of cationic and anionic dyes with > 90% removal efficiencies. This protocol ensures a complete repurposing of organic waste into modified materials via a zero waste process as demonstrated by its schematic life cycle inventory chart.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organic waste
Photoluminescent sensor
Toxic metal ion
Biochar
Dye adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N & S doped fluorescent BCDs from various waste derived sources
Quantum yield (QY) calculations for the BCDs
Sensing of metal ions in real samples
Physicochemical properties of the biogenic carbon dots
Stability of photoluminescence properties of the biogenic carbon dots
Determining the selectivity of BCDs towards metal ions
Assessing sensitivity of BCDRP toward Cr6+metal ions
Assessing sensitivity of BCDBP toward Fe3+metal ions
Assessment of the applicability of synthesized carbon dots in sensing real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to DST-FIST, Department of Chemistry for NMR facility. We are grateful to SAIF-IIT Mumbai for HRTEM and FESEM. MD acknowledges Solvay Research and Innovation Center, Savli, Vadodara for financial support (Grant Number: GCU-4/S.R.F./93/#10154/444). HT is thankful to GUJCOST, Gandhinagar for financial support (Grant Number: GUJCOST/2020-21/881).

The authors are grateful to DST-FIST, Department of Chemistry for NMR facility. We are grateful to SAIF-IIT Mumbai for HRTEM and FESEM. MD acknowledges Solvay Research and Innovation Center , Savli, Vadodara for financial support (Grant Number: GCU-4/S.R.F./93/#10154/444 ). HT is thankful to GUJCOST , Gandhinagar for financial support (Grant Number: GUJCOST/2020-21/881 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 1H NMR spectra of (A) BCDRP and (B) BCDBP.

(A) IR overlay of BCDRP and BCDBP; optical properties of the carbon dots; Imaging by HR-TEM and size distribution graphs from DLS for (B–D) BCDRP and (E–G) BCDBP. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) & (B) Elemental analysis of BCDRP and BCDBP respectively; UV–vis and fluorescence spectra showing excitation and emission wavelengths for (C), (D) BCDRP and (E) (F) BCDBP. Inset pictures of cuvette containing carbon dot solution under daylight and UV irradiation.

Stability studies of the carbon dots under various conditions w.r.t. different (A) irradiation times (B) ionic strength and (C) storage time (excitation wavelength=316 nm (BCDRP) & 330 nm (BCDBP); emission wavelength= 407 nm (BCDRP) & 437 nm (BCDRP)). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Assessment of selectivity of the sensors towards metal ions (A), (B) photographs showing BCDRP and BCDRP solutions in the presence of various metal ions at 10 nM concentration under UV irradiation (C), (D) fluorescence emission spectra of sensor solution in the presence of these metal ions (E) graph showing the relative fluorescence intensities.

(A) Schematic illustration depicting fluorescence quenching of BCDs in the presence of metal ions. Graphs showing quenching of fluorescence emission intensity upon addition of different concentrations of (B) Cr6+ ions in BCDRP at 407 nm of emission wavelength and (C) Fe3+ metal ions in BCDBP at 437 nm of emission wavelength (D) Stern Volmer plot showing linear relationship between FL quenching of BCDRP and Cr6+ ion concentration in the range of 10−100 pM and (E) Stern Volmer plot showing linear relationship between FL quenching of BCDBP and Fe3+ ion concentration in the range of 10−200 pM.
Schematic showing the plausible mechanism for (A) formation of carbon nanodots from the biogenic precursors (B) fluorescence quenching of the carbon dots in presence of the metal ions.

Characterization of the biochar using (A) FTIR spectra (B) elemental analysis and (C) FESEM imaging technique; assessment of the performance of biochar as an adsorbent (D) graph shows % removal efficiency of dye (Conditions: Initial concentration 100 ppm, adsorbent dosage 100 mg at 35 °C).

UV–vis spectra of (A) methylene blue (B) malachite green (C) rhodamine B and methyl orange before and after adsorption; (E) Digital photographs showing the adsorption using the biochar as a column adsorbent.
Schematic demonstrating a life cycle inventory of the organic waste valorization process proposed in this work.
Optimization of the BCD synthesis from various biogenic precursors and their properties.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Size measurements were done using DLS at 37 °C.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: QY was calculated by using Quinine Sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4, Φ = 54%) as standard reference.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of Cr6+ ion sensing using BCDRP in various real samples (n = 3).
Results of Fe3+ ion sensing using BCDBP in various real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Repurposing the domestic organic waste into green emissive carbon dots and carbonized adsorbent: A sustainable zero waste process for metal sensing and dye sequestration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, The Maharaja Sayajirao University of Baroda, Vadodara 390 002, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, The Maharaja Sayajirao University of Baroda

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Interdisciplinary Studies, The Maharaja Sayajirao University of Baroda, Vadodara 390 002, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Interdisciplinary Studies, The Maharaja Sayajirao University of Baroda

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, The Maharaja Sayajirao University of Baroda, Vadodara 390 002, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, The Maharaja Sayajirao University of Baroda

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports a one pot facile route for complete valorization of domestic organic waste into biogenic carbon dots (BCDs) and biochar for application as nanosensor and adsorbent respectively. Among the various precursors used, dried rose petals derived (BCDRP) and ripe banana peel derived (BCDBP) carbon dots demonstrated quantum yields comparable to other previously reported biomass derived green fluorescing carbon dots. The BCDs exhibited sizes < 10 nm and excellent photo-stability upto 30 days with respect to different irradiation times as well as ionic strength. The BCDs were assessed for their optical performance and employed as fluorescent sensor for detection of heavy metal ions. The semi-quantitative and quantitative studies shows that BCDRP was selective towards Cr6+ whereas BCDBP detected Fe3+ in a mixture of metal ions. LOD values in pM range (81 pM for Cr6+ in presence of BCDRP and 121 pM for Fe3+ in presence of BCDBP) advocates their high sensitivity. The real sample analyzing potential of the BCDs with excellent recoveries and minimal RSD suggest their successful practical applicability. For circular economy, the biochar formed as a byproduct was reused as an adsorbent for removal of cationic and anionic dyes with > 90% removal efficiencies. This protocol ensures a complete repurposing of organic waste into modified materials via a zero waste process as demonstrated by its schematic life cycle inventory chart.

ce:section.ce:para: The municipal solid waste generated is composed of wide range of biomolecules in enormous amounts. Few of these compounds that have been identified include vitamins, polyphenols, polysaccharides, proteins, naturally occurring colorants like anthocyanins, lipids, oils etc [1,2]. These molecules are not only enriched sources of carbon but also possess multitude of functional groups viz. hydroxyl, carboxyl, thiol, amino etc [1,3,4]. These functional groups enable the materials to participate in a plethora of complex mechanisms like ion-exchange, adsorption, complexation or chelation. Post suitable valorization that includes chemical or physical modifications these functional materials can undergo morphological and chemical metamorphosis to form compounds for reuse in various interesting applications like sensing and adsorption [5].

There has been a recent advent in the usage of carbon derived photoluminescent nano-materials for sensing applications [6,7]. Amongst various carbonaceous materials, carbon dots are the most promising entities when it comes to application as a sensor due to their size dependent fluorescence behavior and high resistance towards photobleaching [8–10]. Carbon dots were traditionally prepared by complex protocols like electrochemical oxidation/laser ablation of graphite [11–13], pyrolysis of saccharides [14,15], electrochemical oxidation of carbon nanotubes [16], ultrasonic treatment [17] etc. However these processes had their own demerits in terms of photoluminescence intensities, post synthetic passivation and toxicity concerns [18]. This intrigued the researchers to explore synthetic techniques that are not only simpler but also environmentally facile. This quest led to the development of “green carbon dots” prepared by selecting biogenic carbon containing compounds as pre-cursors instead of conventional synthetic chemicals for their preparation. Literature reports have suggested the use of various edible/non-edible green components like rose petal extract [19], milk, fruit juices [20,21], eutrophic algal blooms [22], crab shell, bee pollen, cow manure, human urine, alginates [23], egg-shell membranes ashes [24], mint leaves [25] etc [26]. as precursors for the synthesis of carbon dots. It was also observed that the properties of the biomass derived carbon dots are influenced by the nature of organic compound present in the natural source [27–29].

Wastes generated by the household activities mostly contain biomass derived solid wastes and the increase in the population of quite a lot of urban areas is a common thing in most of the countries and as a result of this, there is a substantial rise in the amount of Municipal Solid Waste generated worldwide. A current generation of about 1.3 billion metric tons of municipal solid waste annually worldwide and as expected increase to 2.2 billion metric tons by 2025 is a proof of this ongoing increase in the population. Uncontrolled combustion/incineration of these wastes has been a common way of managing them in the recent past, which leads to excessive emission of greenhouse gases, affecting the environment at large [30]. Henceforth, developments have been made in the alternative waste management options. Considering these facts, valorization of these carbonaceous wastes is the best possible outcome possible so far. Such domestic organic waste containing mixture of organic compounds, is ideal precursor for preparation of carbon nanodots.

On the other hand, heavy metal ions are amongst the most persistent environmental pollutants due to their non-biodegradability, complexing tendency towards biological ligands and bio-incompatibility [31,32]. With the development of the economy, excess of these metal ions are enriched in the water bodies, thereby accumulating in the living organisms via food chain, eventually leading to several toxic effects to the living organisms. Although Fe3+, being an indispensable trace element in the in biological systems, playing a crucial role in oxygen transport, cellular metabolism, enzyme catalysis, etc., excess concentration of it can cause side effects to organs like liver and kidneys, and brain [31,33,34]. On the other hand, Cr6+ entering the cells via sulfate/phosphate anion transport system in the form of negatively charged oxyanion chromate CrO4 2-, is known to possess potential mutagenic and carcinogenic properties [35–37].

The sensing of metal ions by carbon dots obtained from synthetic precursors has been reported [38]. However, there are very few reports on sensing of heavy metal ions using carbon dots prepared from biogenic sources.

Hence, we attempted the synthesis of emissive carbon dots from waste derived organic sources by a facile one pot hydrothermal route and employed them as fluorescent sensor for detection of heavy metal ions. Thus, this approach can be an effective strategy of valorization of organic waste that is generated on daily basis. Different waste products were sorted and selected as precursors on the basis of the active ingredient they possess. It is noteworthy that the hydrothermal treatment employed for the synthesis of carbon dots generates 2 different entities: the main entity is the supernatant liquid, containing carbon dots, which were doped with N and S heteroatoms, to enhance their optical properties [39,40]. The byproduct, a solid sludge, is basically a biochar. With the ongoing quest of minimizing resource utilization for decreasing the carbon footprints and for designing more sustainable processes, it was rationalized to employ this biochar as an adsorbent without any further chemical modification.

The carbon nanodots from renewable source can be utilized for sensing hazardous entities like metal ions whereas the biochar can assist in waste water remediation by removal of dyes therein. A process, employing organic waste as a precursor for generating materials for sensing and adsorption applications via its complete valorization with minimal carbon footprint has not been reported to far to the best of our knowledge.

Organic domestic waste (dried rose petals, orange peels, ripe banana peels, coffee grounds, used green tea leaves) was collected from various sources. Metal salts (Ferric (III) chloride, potassium dichromate, lead nitrate, mercuric chloride, potassium chloride, sodium chloride, magnesium chloride, copper (II) chloride) and dyes (methylene blue, malachite green, methyl orange and Rhodamine B) were acquired form Spectrochem India. Dopants Ethylene diamine (EDA) and L-Cysteine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All the chemicals were used as received.

For the preparation of all waste extracts, metal ion and dye solutions de-ionized water was used. The real water samples for analysis were collected form cooler, tap and river from the university campus. The purchased fruit juice sample used was of Tropicana Pvt. Ltd. (India) make.
The visual camera pictures were taken using Samsung A51 smartphone in bright lab conditions/daylight condition.

The liquid carbon dot samples were lyophilized using IIshin Bio Base TFD 8503 lyophillizer. The 1H NMR analysis was recorded on Bruker Avance of 400 MHz frequency using tetramethylsilane as an internal standard and D2O as the solvent. FTIR was carried out with a Bruker Alpha IR spectrophotometer in range of 4000–400 cm−1 by placing the sample on CaF2 window at room temperature. DLS analysis was performed on Malvern Zetasizer (Nano-ZS 90). The fluorescence spectra were recorded using Jasco FP-6300 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The UV-Vis spectroscopic analysis was performed using UV-Vis Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies Cary 60). The High resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was performed on Jeol (Jem-2100F) electron microscope operated at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. For the imaging, the solutions of samples were diluted at ambient temperature and then dispersed on a carbon coated copper grid. These grids were air dried at ambient temperature overnight. Elemental mapping was recorded with the EDS attachment on the TEM microscope. FESEM was analyzed using JSM7600F instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N & S doped fluorescent BCDs from various waste derived sources
ce:section.ce:para: The BCDs were synthesized using hydrothermal treatment [41]. Briefly, the biogenic precursors (rose petals, orange peels, ripe banana peels, coffee grounds and used green tea leaves) were first washed thoroughly with deionized water. Rose petals, orange peels, ripe banana peels (10 g each) were shredded into small pieces and dispersed in 300 ml of deionized water and heated to boiling. Coffee grounds and green tea leaves (10 g) were dispersed in 300 ml of deionized water and heated until the solution boils. The solids were removed to obtain filtrates containing the active components and stored in glass vials until further use. The aqueous filtrate, 25 mL, was taken into Teflon lined autoclave. To this solution 300 µL of ethylene diamine and 300 mg of L-Cysteine were added as dopants. The mass was subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 6 h. After 6 h, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the resulting suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to separate the suspended particles. The pale yellow liquid containing carbon dots were separated by decantation and stored in glass vial until further use for sensing experiments. The black sludge obtained as a solid waste was given 3 washings of water and acetone respectively and vacuum dried. The dried powder designated as Biochar was stored in desiccator until further use for dye removal experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield (QY) calculations for the BCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield was calculated as per an established procedure [42]. Briefly, the carbon dots were diluted with deionized water (η = 1.33). Quinine was used as a standard for measuring the QY. Quinine solution was diluted using 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY=54%, η = 1.33). The absorbance and FL spectra or all the carbon dots were recorded at their respective excitation wavelengths. The absorbance value of both the carbon dots and quinine solution was maintained at 0.05 in a 1 cm quartz cuvette. The value of integrated fluorescence intensity obtained for the carbon dot solution was compared to the quinine standard as per the below mentioned equations as a
QY
x
=
QY
std
I
x
A
std
n
x
2
/
I
std
A
x
n
std
2

Here, “I” is the measured integrated emission intensity, “n” is the refractive index of the solvent, and “A” is absorbance. The subscript “std” refers to the standard sample with known QY and “x” for the unknown samples.

In the initial experiments, the selectivity of the metal ions was detected. For this assessment, 100 μL of carbon dots solution was diluted to 1 mL (10 folds dilution). The metal ions selected for the detection were Mg2+, Cu2+, Na+, K+, Cr6+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+. For each of these metal ions, 10 μL of 10 nM concentration, was taken in separate vials and mixed with 100 μL of sensor solution. The resultant mixture was incubated for 3–5 min at room temperature. The fluorescence quenching was confirmed by visual observations by placing the sample vials in UV chamber. Then the fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 407 nm for BCDRP and 437 nm for BCDBP.

For the quantitative and trace level sensing of Cr6+ and Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence studies were performed, using standard stock solutions of 10−6 M concentration. The solutions were further diluted to nM range by serial dilutions and the studies were performed in a range of 0.025–1000 nM for both the sensors and metal ions. Metal ion solutions (10 µL) of the respective dilutions were added to 100 µL of the carbon dot solution and fluorescence spectra was recorded.

Thereafter the determination of LOD and LOQ was also carried out in the similar concentration range by fitting the obtained results into Stern-Volmer plot as per following the equation:
I
0
I
− 1 =
K
SV
C
where “I
o
” is the intensity of BCD solution, “I” represents the intensity of the BCD loaded with the metal ion, “Ksv” is the Stern-Volmer constant and “C” represents the concentration of the Cr6+ and Fe3+ ions for BCDRP and BCDBP respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of metal ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The application of this fluorescence-based sensing method for real samples analysis, such as river water, tap water and packaged fruit juice samples was assessed. The samples were used without any further filtration. 500 μL of each of these samples were spiked with standard solution of Cr6+/Fe3+ and mixed with sensor solution to a final metal ion concentration in the range of 1.0−100 nM. The fluorescence quenching of the samples was measured with fluorescence spectrophotometer. The recoveries were counted as per the following equation:
R = [(Cz − Cy)/Cx] × 100%

where “R” is the % recovery, “Cx” is metal ion concentration added into the water samples, “Cy” is metal concentration in the water samples before spiking, and “Cz” is metal ion concentration in water samples after spiking.

The biochar obtained as sludge was utilized as an adsorbent. Various cationic and anionic dyes such as methylene blue (MB), malachite green (MG), methyl orange (MO) and rhodamine B (RB) were selected for the study. 100 ppm standard solution of the dyes was prepared using deionized water. Bio-char (100 mg) of was added into 50 ml of the dye solution having a concentration of 100 ppm at 35 °C under stirring for 3 h. As a result, there was a visual change seen in the dye solution after the addition of bio-char. The supernatant after adsorption was analyzed using UV–vis spectrophotometry at λmax = 664 for MB, λmax = 617 for MG, λmax = 460 for MO and λmax = 556 for RB respectively. The adsorption % removal efficiency of the dyes was calculated using the formula:
%
R
e
=
C
0
−
C
e
C
e
× 100

The optimization of the most appropriate BCD for sensing application was done by selecting various natural carbon sources. The sources were edible and non-edible carbonaceous wastes generated from daily human activities. The type of active chemical ingredient contained in these wastes was one of the criteria for their selection. Literature reports suggest that introducing atomic dopants like N and S assists in enhancing the fluorescent properties and the quantum yield [43]. Thus during synthesis, the reaction masses of all the BCDs were doped with N and S using ethylene diamine and L-cysteine as the respective doping agents. The BCDs thus synthesized via hydrothermal treatment were subjected to analysis for evaluation of their optical properties and size measurements. The details of synthesized BCDs and their properties have been summarized in Table 1 and Fig. S1.

All the synthesized BCDs revealed respectable QY, comparable with other natural source derived carbon dots reported previously in literature. Amongst the five synthesized BCDs, a higher quantum yield was observed in case of rose petals and banana peel derived CDs. Hence these two systems (BCD
RP
and BCD
BP
) were selected for detailed investigation and for the assessment of their sensing potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of the biogenic carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The structure of N and S doped BCD
RP
and BCD
BP was analyzed using proton nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. The spectra of both BCD
RP and BCD
BP (as shown in Fig. 1) showed characteristic peaks corresponding to sp3 hybridized alkyls groups in the range 0.5−1.75 ppm. Further the sample also showed presence of protons in the alkyl chains attached to carbonyl groups such as aldehyde, ketone or ester. Moreover, the allylic protons (Ar−CH3) were observed in the range of 2.0–3.0 ppm. Protons in the range of 3.0–3.5 ppm is an indicative of the presence of either alkoxy (–OCH3); aryl alkyl ethers (OCH2); alkyl and aryl/alkyl esters. There may be presence of alkene like moiety attached to aryl or alky groups (Ar/R′CH=CH-) as protons corresponding to these functionalities were observed in the range δ = 4.5–6.0 ppm. The appearance of such peaks led to the conclusion that a complete transformation of alkyl hydroxyl groups into alkene groups occurs via dehydration pathways as the samples are being subjected to hydrothermal conditions. Further there is a transformation of alkyl groups into aryl groups evident due to the presence of peaks in the range of 7.0–8.5 ppm [19,26].

The FTIR spectra of carbon dots were recorded for probing the type of functionalities on their surface. Fig. 2A demonstrates the IR spectra overlay for both the BCDRP and BCDBP. A broad peak corresponding to –OH stretching was observed at 3321 cm−1 for both the carbon dots. The vibrations corresponding to C-H and N-H stretching frequencies appeared at 2981 cm−1 in case of BCDRP and at 2977 cm−1 for BCDBP. The bands observed in the range of 1750–1770cm−1 represent –CO stretching frequency. The stretching frequencies for CC groups were observed at 1505–1510 cm−1. Further the C-O stretching frequencies for both the carbon dots were observed in the range of 1070–1080 cm−1. These observations confirm the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxylic acid groups on the surface of the carbon dots that attributes to their stability while in aqueous phase [44].

The size and morphology were assessed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) images of both BCDRP and BCDBP. The images showed distinctly separate spherical particles (marked by pink and yellow circles) (Fig. 2B & E and Fig. S2) suggesting the presence of monodispersed nanoparticles. The size of the nanoparticles was observed to be 6.9 and 9.3 nm for BCDRP and BCDBP respectively.

Further, the lattice fringes with d-spacing of 0.22 and 0.21 nm for observed for BCDRP and BCDBP respectively via HR-TEM images revealed the presence of graphitic carbon having (1 0 0) lattice plane (Fig. 2C & F). The size distribution observed in DLS measurements corroborate with the HRTEM observations (Fig. 2D &G).

The elemental mapping performed using TEM microscope revealed the presence of C, H, N, O and S in the constitution of both the carbon dots thus confirming the observations of the FTIR measurements and elemental composition ( Fig. 3A &B). The percentage of C, N, O and S in BCDRP was found to be 45.97, 28.48, 21.88, 1.49 + 0.38 + 0.38 + 0.74 + 0.19 + 0.87. Similarly, the percentage of C, N, O and S in BCDRP was found to be 47.28, 25.84, 22.67, 3.13 + 0.25 + 0.42 + 0.15 + 0.23 + 0.87 + 0.25 respectively.

The assessment of optical properties was performed using UV–vis spectroscopic determinations. A broad absorption band was obtained in the range of 250–380 nm for BCDRP and 270–400 nm for BCDBP (Fig. 3C &E). A small peak that occurs in the range of 250–280 nm is expected to appear owing to the π-π* transitions that occur due to the presence of conjugated CC bonds in the composition of carbon dots. Further the peak originating in the range of 310–380 nm is due to the n-π* energy transitions of carbonyl group. This n-π* transition is also responsible for the emission of fluorescence from the carbon dots [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of photoluminescence properties of the biogenic carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The PL stability of both sensor solutions was examined by comparison of their fluorescence intensities under various experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 3A, when the solution of carbon dots is irradiated continuously for 1 h there is no significant change in the emission intensity. Further the solutions also did not show any photobleaching or coagulation post irradiation which is indicative of good photostability [46]. The effect of ionic strength on the emission intensity has been shown in Fig. 3B. The results demonstrated that increasing the concentration of NaCl from 0 nM to 1000 nM did not induce any effect on the fluorescence property. This is suggestive of the fact that the carbon dot solution is unlikely to undergo any salt interference and NaCl of different ionic strength can aid in controlling the aggregation of the carbon dots as no precipitation is observed even upon addition of 1000 nM of NaCl.

Fig. 3C demonstrates that the sensor solution remains stable upto 30 days as the PL properties remained unchanged. The solutions were observed to be visibly clear and devoid of any floaters or precipitates [47,48]. These studies confirm the excellent photostability of both the biogenic carbon dots advocating their potential for application as nanosensors.

The fluorescent properties of the carbon dots were further analyzed by photoluminescence studies. As shown in Fig. 4D&F, upon optimum excitation at 316 nm and 330 nm BCDRP and BCDBP showed intense peak corresponding to emission at 407 nm and 437 nm respectively. Thus the solution containing carbon dots appear pale yellow in color in daylight and green fluorescence under UV light (inset image Fig. 4C–F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determining the selectivity of BCDs towards metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of COOH, OH, NH, SH and CO groups, on the BCDs suggested possibility of interactions with the metal ions [49]. Thus, the sensing ability and selectivity of BCDs in the presence of eight different metal ions (Fe3+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Cr6+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+) was assessed by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots. Also, the fluorescence quenching of BCDBP was also compared in presence between Fe3+ and Fe2+ was assessed.

The variations occurring in the fluorescence emission intensity (F0/F) at 407 and 437 nm for BCD
RP and BCD
BP respectively were determined. The fluorescence quenching by the samples was observed visually by irradiation of the sensor-analyte mixture in a UV chamber (as shown in Fig. 4A & B). Also, the visual change in the fluorescence quenching of the BCDBP in presence of Fe3+ and Fe2+ was observed in the UV chamber (Fig. S4). The changes in the PL intensity in the presence of each of these metal ions at 10 nM concentration post 5 min of incubation is shown in Fig. 5C&D. Both the visual readout and PL studies depicted that BCD
RP experienced quenching in presence of Cr6+ metal ions. On the other hand, addition of Fe3+ caused a quenching in fluorescence of BCD
BP . Interestingly, quenching of BCDBP wasn’t observed in the presence of Fe2+ ions. This suggests the specificity of BCD
RP and BCD
BP towards sensing of Cr6+ and Fe3+ respectively.

This was further confirmed by plotting a histogram of (F0/F-1) versus the metal ions at 10 nM concentration (Fig. 5E). The comparison of the PL intensity of blank sensor and sensor after analyte addition helped in the evaluation of the sensing potency. It was observed that a significant alteration in the emission intensity occurred only in the presence of Cr6+ in BCD
RP solution and Fe3+ in BCD
BP solution as compared to the other metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessing sensitivity of BCDRP toward Cr6+metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6A illustrates the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching (as per digital photographs) in the presence of metal ions. For further analyzing the sensitivity of the rose petal derived carbon dot in detecting Cr6+ ions, the sensor solution was added to different solutions having varying concentration of the metal ion (0.025–1000 nM). The blank sensor solution showed strong PL intensity peak at 407 nm which reduces by ~50% of the initial intensity on adding merely 0.075 nM of Cr6+ solution (Fig. 6B). There is further decrement in the emission intensity on increasing the concentration of metal ion. An incubation of the sensor with metal ions for a time interval of 5 min results in total quenching of the fluorescence.

The Stern-Volmer plots of (F0/F-1) vs. concentration of metal ions in the range of 0.025–0.1 nm showed good linearity and a correlation coefficient value of R2 = 0.9952 (Fig. 6D). The limit of detection was found to be 81 pM, thus indicating a high sensitivity of BCD
RP for Cr6+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessing sensitivity of BCDBP toward Fe3+metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: A similar study was carried out for determining the sensitivity of the ripe banana peel derived carbon dot for detecting Fe3+ ions. The sensor solution was added to different solutions having varying concentration in the range of 0.025–1000 nM. In this case, the blank sensor solution showed strong emission peak at 437 nm which reduces on sequentially increasing the metal ion concentration (Fig. 6C).

The Stern-Volmer plots of (F0/F-1) vs. concentration of metal ions in the range of 0.025–0.25 nm showed good linearity and a correlation coefficient value of R2 = 0.9960 (Fig. 6E). The limit of detection was found to be 121 pM, showing a high sensitivity of BCD
BP for Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the applicability of synthesized carbon dots in sensing real samples
ce:section.ce:para: An assessment of the ability of these biogenic sensors in sensing Cr6+ and Fe3+ was carried out in real samples viz. water, food. The PL spectra of all these samples did not show the presence of either of the metal ions hence for the evaluation purpose standard addition method was adapted.

The collected samples were spiked with known concentrations of Cr6+ and the fluorescence intensity was measured. Upon addition of BCDRP sensor solution a quenching of fluorescence was observed. The measurements were recorded in triplicate and reported in terms of relative standard deviation (as shown in Table 2). A similar protocol was followed for detection of Fe3+ spiked samples using BCDBP. The measurements have been mentioned in Table 3.

Both the sensors demonstrated a % recovery in the range of 94.5–102.3% with relative standard deviations less than 5% thus establishing the potential of the nanosensors in detection of real samples with good accuracy.

The formation of carbon nanodots from the precursors proceed via a sequential pathway as shown in Fig. 7A. It is initiated with the carbonization of the active component (polysaccharide or anthocyanin). This is followed by an occurrence of decomposition on exposure to hydrothermal treatment [23,50]. The added dopants participate in the process of decomposition that occurs by two steps: Hydrolysis that is L-cysteine assisted followed by dehydration promoted by ethylene diamine. This leads to the formation of sugars having C5/C6 in their structures and furan. Polymerization and polycondensation occurs next followed subsequently by N and S enriched aromatization and carbonization. When the aromatic clusters attain a critical concentration, N and S co-doped biogenic carbon dots are formed due to nuclear burst [51].

The plausible mechanism has been proposed for the detection of the metal ions using both BCDRP and BCDBP has been illustrated in Fig. 7B. The quenching can be attributed to the formation of a non-fluorescing compound/complex upon interaction of carbon dots with the metal ions [52]. Particularly, Fe3+ and Cr6+ shows the most obvious quenching effect on the PL intensity of BCDBP and BCDRP respectively amongst the various metal ions used and this is probably because of higher thermodynamic affinity and faster chelating of Fe3+ and Cr6+ towards “N” and “O” of BCDs than other transition-metal ions. Owing to the N/S doping, the formation of coordination bonds between metal ions and the functional groups on the surface of BCDs become much easier, which is pretty much consistent. Thus, leading to the formation of a non-fluorescing compound/complex upon interaction of carbon dots with the metal ions, with the electron in the exited state of BCDs being transferred to the half-filled 3d orbits of Fe3+ and Cr6+ inducing nonradiative electron/hole recombination and annihilation, which leads to the fluorescence quenching(refences). This is evident from the FTIR spectra wherein the peaks corresponding to –OH/NH, SH, CO functionalities at 3345 cm−1 2525 cm−1 and 1751 cm−13 exhibit weaker intensities due to their interaction with metal ions (Fig. S3A) [53]. This interaction results in the formation of larger aggregates which is observed in HRTEM images of the sensor solutions incubated with metal ions (Fig. S3 B &D). The formation of larger aggregates also aids in the fluorescence quenching [54]. The elemental analysis also showed presence of Cr in BCDRP sample and Fe in BCDBP sample suggestive of interaction between sensor and metal ions (Fig. S3 C & E).

The biochar generated was used as a valorized product for adsorption of dyes from water at neutral pH conditions. Out of both the precursors, the biochar of ripe banana peel was used for experiments. Prior to its application, the adsorbent was subjected to analytical characterization. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 8A showed peaks corresponding to hydroxyl groups at 3343 cm−1, CO stretching frequency was observed at 1732cm−1 and CC peaks were obtained at 1608cm−1 [55]. The elemental analysis corroborated with the FTIR data and showed presence of 73.16% of elemental C and 23.84% of elemental O (Fig. 8B).

The SEM image showed the presence of crevices on the biochar surface (Fig. 8C). These crevices are generated as the particles crack as a result of volatile compounds emanating from the pyrolyzed biomass [56]. These characterizations suggest that the presence of functional groups and crevices on the biochar can support its functioning as an adsorbent.

For the adsorption experiments 4 different dyes MB, MG, MO and RB were selected as model cationic and anionic dyes. The biochar 100 mg was added to 50 mL of dye solutions having 100 ppm concentration. The solution was stirred at 40 rpm on a magnetic stirrer at 35 °C. The equilibrium time for adsorption was found to be 3 h. A rapid uptake of dye was observed with > 90% of dye removal in all cases (Fig. 8D). Post 3 h, the adsorbent was removed and the supernatant was measured via UV–vis spectrophotometry at the respective emission wavelengths of the dyes to calculate the removal efficiency. Fig. 9A–D suggests excellent removal of all the dyes from the UV–vis spectra as well as from the digital photographs of the analytes (inset Fig. 9A–D). For the ease of dye removal, the efficacy of the biochar as a column adsorbent was also assessed. The experiments were performed in a glass column as shown in (Fig. 9E).
Thus, the results are suggestive of the capability of the biochar to function as adsorbent for waste water remediation.

As per life cycle inventory shown in Fig. 10, two system boundaries can be identified; one which is associated with the emissions from the hydrothermal treatment and second from the energy requirement (electricity) for the treatment [57]. As a result of the hydrothermal treatment, two fractions were obtained: the filtrate containing BCDs and the retentate containing bio-char, both having their own applications. Once, both of these have been used to its fullest potential, they can be discarded via incineration or in the form of landfills, thereby making the entire process energy efficient and a zero-carbon footprint process.

Thus, to summarize, this work is an attempt to reduce the carbon footprint burden by utilization of domestic organic waste for developing materials for crucial applications like sensing and remediation. The hydrothermal treatment using renewable waste derived source yields carbon nanodots which is the major product of this reaction. The waste sludge generated on the other hand was further reused as a biochar adsorbent. The biogenic carbon dots are green emissive fluorescent probe and were successfully applied for selective pM detection of Cr6+ and Fe3+ metal ions with LODs in pM range (81 pM for BCDRP and 121 pM for BCDBP). The potential for sensing real samples was also analyzed with excellent recoveries and minimal RSD suggesting their successful practical applicability. The biochar was reused as such without any further chemical modification as an adsorbent for dye removal with > 90% removal efficiencies for both cationic and anionic dyes. The methodology thus demonstrated a complete valorization of the waste and efficient usage of both products and process. This approach is thus a sustainable and environmentally amicable for both metal sensing as well as environmental remediation via a single process. This work proposes an effective strategy for preparation of green emissive BCDs and also provides the photo-luminescence mechanism of BCDs and quenching of fluorescence in presence of the metal ions. In addition, the efficiency of the BCDs to detect metal ions can prove this method to be a sustainable candidate in detection of the metal ions in biological samples as well. Apart from this, future attempts can be made to develop red emissive biogenic carbon dots (BCDs) which can have several applications in biological field due to their less interference by the autofluorescence of biological matrix and lower photodamage to biological tissues.

Manita Das: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Harshil Thakkar: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Divyesh Patel: Supervision, Data curation. Sonal Thakore: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to DST-FIST, Department of Chemistry for NMR facility. We are grateful to SAIF-IIT Mumbai for HRTEM and FESEM. MD acknowledges Solvay Research and Innovation Center, Savli, Vadodara for financial support (Grant Number: GCU-4/S.R.F./93/#10154/444). HT is thankful to GUJCOST, Gandhinagar for financial support (Grant Number: GUJCOST/2020-21/881).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2021.106312.

ce:section.ce:para:
